note: 30-word γ pilot. Read each cluster, emit verdict in strict JSON (see GAMMA_SYSTEM_PROMPT).

senses.text: the deliberate ending of a pregnancy at an early stage
examples: to support/oppose abortion
a woman’s right to abortion
the country's strict abortion laws
Both pro-choice and anti-abortion groups want to ensure their voices are heard.

senses.text: a medical operation to end a pregnancy at an early stage
examples: She decided to have an abortion.
Some medical staff wanted the right to refuse to perform abortions.

senses.text: not in a place because of illness, etc.
examples: She was absent from work for two weeks.
Local people were conspicuously absent from the meeting.

senses.text: not present in something
examples: Love was totally absent from his childhood.
She now played with a passion that had been strangely absent from her previous performance.

senses.text: to take in a liquid, gas or other substance from the surface or space around
examples: Plants absorb carbon dioxide from the air.
Let the rice cook until it has absorbed all the water.
The cream is easily absorbed into the skin.

senses.text: to take in and keep heat, light, energy, sound, etc. instead of reflecting it
examples: Black walls absorb a lot of heat during the day.

senses.text: based on general ideas and not on any particular real person, thing or situation
examples: abstract knowledge/principles
The research shows that pre-school children are capable of thinking in abstract terms.

senses.text: existing in thought or as an idea but not having a physical reality
examples: We may talk of beautiful things but beauty itself is abstract.

senses.text: not representing people or things in a realistic way, but expressing the artist’s ideas about them
examples: the work of American abstract expressionists like Mark Rothko

senses.text: the opportunity or right to use something or to see somebody/something
examples: High-speed internet access has become a necessity.
You need a password to get access to the computer system.
access to information/services/resources
access to healthcare/education
They are seeking increased access to markets overseas.

senses.text: a way of entering or reaching a place
examples: The police gained access through a broken window.
Many paths have become overgrown, blocking access.
There is easy access by road.
We welcome disabled visitors and there is wheelchair access to all parts of the park.
The only access to the farmhouse is across the fields.

senses.text: a place to live, work or stay in
examples: rented/temporary accommodation
Hotel accommodation is included in the price of your holiday.
We may have to provide alternative accommodation for you.
First-class accommodation is available on all flights.
It can be difficult to find suitable accommodation for those in need.

senses.text: somewhere to live or stay, often also providing food or other services
examples: More and more travelers are looking for bed and breakfast accommodations in private homes.
The boat provides reasonable overnight accommodations for four adults.

senses.text: to gradually get more and more of something over a period of time
examples: I seem to have accumulated a lot of books.
By investing wisely she accumulated a fortune.
We have accumulated a great amount of evidence.

senses.text: to gradually increase in number or quantity over a period of time
examples: Debts began to accumulate.
Dust and dirt soon accumulate if a house is not cleaned regularly.

senses.text: a long narrow piece of paper, metal, cloth, etc.
examples: a strip of material
Cut the meat into strips.

senses.text: a long narrow area of land, sea, etc.
examples: the Gaza Strip
a tiny strip of garden
The islands are separated by a narrow strip of water.

senses.text: connected with the ideas and principles on which a particular subject is based, rather than with practice and experiment
examples: a theoretical approach
theoretical physics
The first year provides students with a sound theoretical basis for later study.

senses.text: that could possibly exist, happen or be true, although this is unlikely
examples: It's a theoretical possibility.

senses.text: making you feel shy, uncomfortable or ashamed
examples: an embarrassing moment/situation
an embarrassing mistake/gaffe
an embarrassing question
I didn't want to risk making the rest of the evening painfully embarrassing for both of us.
His terrible acting is just embarrassing to watch.

senses.text: causing somebody to look stupid, dishonest, etc.
examples: This EU ruling puts Britain in a very embarrassing position.
to be potentially/politically embarrassing
The report is likely to prove highly embarrassing to the government.
Such action by a NATO ally would be extremely embarrassing for the United States.

senses.text: freedom from political control by other countries
examples: Argentina gained independence from Spain in 1816.

senses.text: the time when a country gains freedom from political control by another country
examples: independence celebrations
the first elections since independence

senses.text: the freedom to organize your own life, make your own decisions, etc. without needing help from other people
examples: He values his independence.
Financial independence was imprortant to her.

senses.text: to move suddenly and with one quick movement in a particular direction
examples: The cat crouched ready to spring.
He turned off the alarm and sprang out of bed.
She sprang back in alarm.
Eric sprang forward and tackled the man to the ground.
Everyone sprang to their feet (= stood up suddenly) when the principal walked in.

senses.text: to move suddenly and violently
examples: The branch sprang back and hit him in the face.
She turned the key and the lid sprang open.

senses.text: to look closely at something/somebody, especially to check that everything is as it should be
examples: The teacher walked around inspecting their work.
Make sure you inspect the goods before signing for them.
The plants are regularly inspected for disease.

senses.text: to officially visit a school, factory, etc. in order to check that rules are being obeyed and that standards are acceptable
examples: Public health officials were called in to inspect the premises.
The Tourist Board inspects all recommended hotels at least once a year.

senses.text: a place where trains stop so that passengers can get on and off; the buildings connected with this
examples: the main station
Euston Station
a train station
a railway station
a tube/an underground station

senses.text: a place where buses stop; the buildings connected with this
examples: We waited for him at the bus station.
a coach station
Victoria Coach Station

senses.text: the bottom surface of the foot
examples: The hot sand burned the soles of their feet.

senses.text: the bottom part of a shoe or sock, not including the heel
examples: leather soles
I’ve got a hole in the sole of my shoe.

senses.text: that cannot be found or that is not in its usual place; that has been removed, lost or destroyed
examples: I never found the missing piece.
Cases with missing data are excluded from the report.
My gloves have been missing for ages.
Two files have gone missing.
Our cat's gone missing again.

senses.text: not at home; not found
examples: a missing four-year-old girl
They still hoped to find their missing son.
a woman who went missing three months ago
Posters of missing persons lined the walls.

senses.text: not present after an accident, battle, etc. but not known to have been killed
examples: He was reported missing, presumed dead.
Many soldiers were listed as missing in action.
It was six hours before the seamen were discovered missing.

senses.text: not included, often when it should have been
examples: Fill in the missing words in this text.
There were several candidates missing from the list.
What is missing from the production is any sense of emotional commitment.

senses.text: a thing that is made of several parts, especially a building
examples: a stone/brick/wooden structure
She used sand and rocks to build elaborate structures on the beach.

senses.text: the state of being well organized or planned with all the parts linked together; a careful plan
examples: Your essay needs (a) structure.

senses.text: having warm or loving feelings for somebody, especially somebody you have known for a long time
examples: Over the years, I have grown quite fond of her.
I’ve always been very fond of your mother.

senses.text: finding something pleasant, especially something you have liked or enjoyed for a long time
examples: fond of music/cooking
We had grown fond of the house and didn't want to leave.

senses.text: a person, a situation, an event, etc. that is very similar to another, especially one in a different place or time
examples: These ideas have parallels in Freud's thought too.
This tradition has no parallel in our culture.
This is an achievement without parallel in modern times.

senses.text: similar features
examples: There are interesting parallels between the 1960s and the late 1990s.
It is possible to draw a parallel between (= find similar features in) their experience and ours.

senses.text: to hit somebody/something quickly and lightly
examples: Someone tapped at the door.
He was busy tapping away at his computer.
Ralph tapped me on the shoulder.
Tap the icon to open the app.

senses.text: if you tap your fingers, feet, etc. or they tap, you hit them gently against a table, the floor, etc., for example to the rhythm of music
examples: He kept tapping his fingers on the table.
The music set everyone's feet tapping.

senses.text: a large number of cases of a particular disease or medical condition happening at the same time in a particular community
examples: the outbreak of a flu epidemic
An epidemic of measles broke out, and over 200 children died.
In Victorian times, London endured regular epidemics of smallpox and cholera.

senses.text: a sudden rapid increase in how often something bad happens
examples: A recent report describes an epidemic of crime in the inner cities.

senses.text: the feeling that somebody/something is good or acceptable; a positive opinion of somebody/something
examples: She desperately wanted to win her parents' approval.
Do the plans meet with your approval?
Several people nodded in approval.
The president's approval ratings were slipping.

senses.text: agreement to, or permission for something, especially a plan or request
examples: The plan will be submitted to the committee for official approval.
parliamentary/congressional/government approval
Senior management have given their seal of approval (= formal approval) to the plans.
I can't agree to anything without my partner's approval.
planning approvals

senses.text: to move through water by moving your arms and legs, without touching the bottom
examples: I can't swim.
The boys swam across the lake.
We swam out (= away from land) to the yacht.
They spent the day swimming and sunbathing.
I've only just learned to swim.

senses.text: to spend time swimming for pleasure
examples: I go swimming twice a week.
We always went swimming together in our indoor pool.

senses.text: to move through or across water
examples: Sharks must swim or die.
A shoal of fish swam past.
Ducks were swimming around on the river.
A beaver swam vigorously upstream.

senses.text: the fact of somebody dying or being killed
examples: the anniversary of his wife’s death
his sudden/untimely/premature death
the tragic death of a child
The jury returned a verdict of accidental death.
It is believed she died a violent death.

senses.text: the end of life; the state of being dead
examples: Police are trying to establish the cause of death.
The disease can cause death unless the patient is treated promptly.
Do you believe in life after death?
He was sentenced to death (= to be executed).
Millions of people starved to death (= were killed by lack of food).
